claim: IPCC AR6 identifies South Asia as a global hotspot for extreme heat, with wet-bulb temperatures approaching 35°C in parts of the Indo-Gangetic Plain and coastal Pakistan during extreme events.

source.title: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 11: Weather and Climate Extreme Events

method: Reviewed extreme heat section, regional projections, and wet-bulb temperature analysis.
limitations: Global climate model resolution may not fully capture urban heat island effects.
Wet-bulb projections depend on humidity modeling with substantial uncertainty.

claim: A 2017 Science Advances study found that the 2015 India-Pakistan heatwave was the fifth deadliest globally since 1900, with excess mortality concentrated in urban areas among outdoor workers and elderly.

source.title: Increasing probability of mortality during Indian heat waves

method: Reviewed mortality analysis, heatwave attribution, and vulnerability factors.
limitations: Mortality data is incomplete and may undercount heat-related deaths.
Causal attribution to heat versus underlying conditions is challenging.

claim: The 2014 PLOS Medicine study documenting development of South Asia's first heat-health action plan in Ahmedabad found that the 2010 HAP was associated with a reduction in all-cause mortality during a subsequent heat event, demonstrating that urban heat adaptation interventions are implementable in a South Asian megacity context.

source.title: Development and Implementation of South Asia's First Heat-Health Action Plan in Ahmedabad, Gujarat, India

method: Reviewed study design, intervention components, mortality comparison methodology, and implementation constraints.
limitations: Mortality reduction is estimated from comparison years, not from a randomized trial — confounding from concurrent interventions cannot be ruled out.
Results are from one city; generalizability to cities with different governance, health system capacity, and population characteristics is uncertain.

Original NRDC resource URL (https://www.nrdc.org/resources/ahmedabad-heat-action-plan) was found to return HTTP 404 during verify:sources on 2026-06-07 and was replaced with this primary study reference.
